one_line_takeaway: Argus reorchestrates SAST around LLM agents (RAG-based supply-chain sink discovery + a CodeQL-anchored Retrieval-Recursion-Review data-flow pipeline) and, on seven real Java repos, went from CodeQL/IRIS finding 0-1 vulnerabilities per repo to Argus finding several true vulnerabilities per repo (up to 6), including new zero-day flows tied to prior CVEs, at a reported average cost of ~$2.54 and ~0.44 hours per repository.
key_finding: On the end-to-end evaluation (Table 2), CodeQL detected 0 vulnerabilities across all seven repos (sink counts not reported, shown as '-'); IRIS detected large sink counts (e.g., 5,840 sinks for PublicCMS, 13,962 for KeyCloak) but only 0 or 1 vulnerabilities per repo (1 vulnerability only for JSPWiki); Argus, with either Claude-Sonnet-3.5 or Claude-Sonnet-4.5 as backbone, produced identical results across both backbones and found far fewer but far more precise sinks (e.g., 8 sinks/6 vulns for PublicCMS, 19 sinks/2 vulns for KeyCloak). Token consumption (Table 3) was higher for Argus than IRIS in most repos (e.g., KeyCloak: 442,095 tokens for Argus vs 57,641 for IRIS) but Argus found substantially more true vulnerabilities per repo. The ablation (Table 4) shows RAG-derived extra sinks contributed additional true vulnerabilities beyond what CodeQL-only ('bare Argus') sink discovery found (e.g., PublicCMS: 5/7 from bare sinks plus 1/1 extra from RAG; DataGear: 3/10 bare plus 3/1 extra from RAG). Reported operational cost: ~0.44 hours and ~$2.54 average per repository scan (Appendix A). The case studies report that Argus's RAG agents surfaced a previously fixed CVE (CVE-2024-37759) and, by reusing its access paths, found two new unpatched, exploitable data flows in DataGear; a second case shows an initially misidentified sink for CVE-2025-31672 in Apache POI still served as an effective seed that the Recursion step used to locate the true sink (DocToHtmlUtils.excelToHtml). The paper states Argus discovered zero-day vulnerabilities that received CVE assignments, though it does not tabulate a complete list of these CVEs in the extracted text beyond the two discussed in the case studies.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Argus, an LLM-agent-centered Static Application Security Testing (SAST) framework for Java codebases that reorganizes the SAST pipeline so LLM agents (not symbolic tools) are the primary orchestrators, with CodeQL and vulnerability databases as supporting inputs. Argus has two components: (1) a RAG-enhanced full supply-chain sink-scanning module that parses project dependency manifests (e.g., pom.xml), queries authoritative sources (NVD, OSV, GHSA, Snyk) and community sources (repo/fork issues) for known vulnerabilities in dependencies, and uses a dedicated PoC-generation agent (following the ReAct paradigm) to iteratively verify exploitability of retrieved candidate vulnerabilities; and (2) a data-flow analysis module the authors call Re3 (Retrieval, Recursion, Review): CodeQL forward search first tries to connect sources to sinks; sinks CodeQL cannot reach are handled by a backward-then-forward 'Recursion' step that walks upstream callers of the sink to build surrogate sinks, whose forward flows are stitched back into candidate paths; a final LLM 'Review' agent audits each candidate flow through end-to-end reachability analysis, hop-by-hop taint/sanitization checking, and structured report export for human review.
threat_model: The paper targets vulnerability discovery in real-world Java source code repositories and their third-party dependencies (supply chain). Sources are untrusted inputs (HTTP request parameters, uploaded files, environment variables, database inputs); sinks are dangerous operations (e.g., SQL execution, system command execution, XML/OOXML parsing calls). The concrete worked example is command injection via subprocess.call(..., shell=True) fed by an unsanitized HTTP 'ip' parameter (Figure 1, illustrative, not from the evaluated codebases). No adversarial/attack threat model against the LLM agents themselves is discussed; the focus is entirely on using the multi-agent system as a defensive vulnerability-discovery tool.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) Argus performs only static taint analysis and does not incorporate dynamic techniques such as fuzzing; (2) despite the agentic design, the multi-agent framework is not fine-tuned (e.g., via reinforcement learning with rewards for sink-discovery accuracy or data-flow completeness) and the authors flag this as future work. Additional caveats evident from the paper itself: evaluation is restricted to Java codebases only (the authors state the method could extend to other languages by building new dependency-analysis agents and CodeQL query templates, but this is untested); baselines are limited to one LLM-assisted tool (IRIS) and one symbolic tool (CodeQL) rather than a broader comparison set; Argus's results were identical for two different LLM backbones (Claude-3.5-Sonnet vs Claude-4.5-Sonnet) in Table 2, which the paper frames as evidence of backbone-robustness but which is also consistent with the pipeline being bottlenecked by non-LLM components (CodeQL retrieval/Recursion) rather than by LLM reasoning quality; token consumption is meaningfully higher than the IRIS baseline in most repos.

quantitative_results: Table 2 end-to-end: CodeQL detected 0 vulnerabilities on all 7 repos (sink counts not reported)
Table 2 end-to-end: IRIS sinks/vulns -- PublicCMS 5840/0, JeecgBoot 8350/0, Ruoyi 980/0, JSPWiki 1828/1, DataGear 237/0, Yudao-Cloud 2184/0, KeyCloak 13962/0
Table 2 end-to-end: Argus (both Claude-Sonnet-3.5 and Claude-Sonnet-4.5) sinks/vulns -- PublicCMS 8/6, JeecgBoot 9/3, Ruoyi 3/3, JSPWiki 7/5, DataGear 11/3, Yudao-Cloud 9/1, KeyCloak 19/2
Table 3 token consumption / vulns -- Argus: PublicCMS 194,538 tokens/6 vulns, JeecgBoot 254,392/3, Ruoyi 32,836/3, JSPWiki 67,605/5, DataGear 9,301/3, Yudao-Cloud 85,661/1, KeyCloak 442,095/2
Table 3 token consumption / vulns -- IRIS: PublicCMS 35,500/0, JeecgBoot 42,400/0, Ruoyi 22,300/0, JSPWiki 24,638/1, DataGear 20,300/0, Yudao-Cloud 25,600/0, KeyCloak 57,641/0

Table 4 ablation (vuln/sinks) bare Argus vs extra-with-RAG -- PublicCMS 5/7 vs 1/1, JeecgBoot 3/9 vs 0/0, Ruoyi 3/3 vs 0/0, JSPWiki 5/7 vs 0/0, DataGear 3/10 vs 3/1, Yudao-Cloud 0/0 vs 0/0, KeyCloak 2/17 vs 0/2
Average runtime and cost per repository scan: ~0.44 hours, ~$2.54 (Appendix A)
baselines_compared: IRIS (Li et al., 2025) -- described by the authors as the only Java-oriented LLM-assisted vulnerability detection framework
